description: A common bird throughout Belize, the Melodious Blackbird is often seen in towns, villages, and farmland. Males are entirely black with a glossy sheen, while females are duller brown-black.
funFact: Named for its melodious, flute-like song, often heard at dawn and dusk.

description: One of the most common hummingbirds in Central America. It has a green body, rufous tail, and straight red bill with a black tip.
funFact: These tiny birds can beat their wings up to 80 times per second!

description: A bold and noisy flycatcher often found in pairs or small groups. Has a distinctive white eyebrow, dark cap, and yellow belly.
funFact: Social Flycatchers build their nests near wasp nests for protection from predators.

description: A large, noisy jay with brown plumage, a pale belly, and a long tail. Often travels in small flocks through the forest canopy.
funFact: Brown Jays are highly social and live in cooperative family groups.

description: A small seedeater with sexual dimorphism: males have a distinctive black hood and white collar, while females are plain buffy-brown.
funFact: Males sing from exposed perches to attract females during breeding season.

description: A boldly patterned wren with heavy black spotting on white underparts and a distinctive barred tail often held upright.
funFact: This wren's loud, musical song is often performed as a duet between mated pairs.

description: The national bird of Belize! Unmistakable with its massive rainbow-colored bill, black plumage, yellow throat and chest, and red undertail.
funFact: Despite its large size, the bill is surprisingly light, made of hollow bone struts covered in keratin.

description: A skulking bird of the forest understory. Males are dull red with a brighter red throat patch, while females are olive-brown.
funFact: Often follows army ant swarms to catch insects fleeing from the ants.

description: A smaller relative of the toucan with a colorful bill, black head, yellow underparts with a black chest band, and red rump.
funFact: Aracaris often sleep communally, with up to six birds roosting together in a tree cavity.

description: A small, compact dove. Males are ruddy brown with a gray crown, while females are more uniformly gray-brown. Often seen on the ground.
funFact: This dove's soft, monotonous cooing is a common sound in open areas throughout Belize.

description: A tiny, colorful finch. Males have blue-black upperparts with a bright yellow forehead and underparts, while females are olive-green.
funFact: Euphonias are specialist mistletoe berry eaters and help spread these parasitic plants.

description: A large, thick-billed songbird with a distinctive black head, white throat, and olive-green back. Often heard singing from cover.
funFact: The name 'saltator' comes from Latin meaning 'dancer' or 'leaper'.

description: A tiny grassland bird. Males are entirely glossy blue-black, while females are plain brown. Males perform spectacular jumping displays.
funFact: Males leap straight up into the air while singing, sometimes jumping over 100 times per hour!

description: An all-black bird with a distinctive long tail and grooved, parrot-like bill. Often seen in small groups in open country.
funFact: Anis are cooperative breeders, with multiple females laying eggs in a single communal nest.

description: A small, mostly green parakeet with an olive throat and blue in the wings. Often seen in noisy flocks flying overhead.
funFact: These parakeets excavate nest cavities in termite mounds in trees.

description: A pale blue-gray tanager with distinctive yellow wing patches visible in flight. Often found in pairs or small groups.
funFact: This tanager often follows mixed-species feeding flocks through the forest.

description: A large, slender cuckoo with rufous upperparts, gray underparts, and a very long tail with white-tipped feathers.
funFact: Named for its squirrel-like movements as it hops through branches hunting for large insects.

description: A brown woodcreeper with heavy streaking on the head and underparts, and a pale ivory-colored, slightly curved bill.
funFact: Woodcreepers use their stiff tail feathers as a prop while climbing tree trunks.

description: A distinctive bird with pale gray plumage, black face and cap, red bare skin around the eye, and a pink-based bill.
funFact: Tityras nest in old woodpecker holes and often line their nests with leaves and bark.

description: A large flycatcher with a massive black bill, rufous-edged wings and tail, and yellow underparts. Similar to Social Flycatcher but larger.
funFact: The huge bill is an adaptation for catching large flying insects and even small fruits.

description: A small bird with a gray-blue crown and upper face, olive-green upperparts, whitish chin, pale gray throat, very pale lemon-yellow breast, and whitish belly with greenish yellow flanks.
funFact: Often joins mixed-species feeding flocks and sometimes hangs upside-down to feed.

description: A small flycatcher with a dark blackish gray crown, white eye-ring, sooty olive-gray back, dusky wings with two thin wing bars, white throat and chin, and white to yellowish white underparts.
funFact: Sits erect on exposed perches and catches insects in mid-air, returning to the same perch and shivers its tail upon landing.

description: Males have slate-black head, neck, and chest with a sky-blue eye ring, bright metallic bluish green to golden green upperparts, and cadmium to chrome yellow underparts. Females have dark slate upperparts instead of metallic green.
funFact: Nests in active termite mounds, with both parents excavating the nest cavity in termitaria.

description: Adults have a light bluish head and underparts, with darker blue upperparts and a shoulder patch colored a different shade of blue. Sexes are similar, but immature birds are much duller.
funFact: Often called blue jean in Trinidad and Tobago, this confiding species thrives around human habitation.

description: Adults have metallic bronze to bronzy green upperparts, with white underparts and metallic bronzy green from the cheeks to the flanks. The tail is metallic bronze with darker bands.
funFact: Males sing in leks and are believed to be polygynous, with females building cup nests decorated with lichen and moss.

description: Males have a yellow forehead, olive-green crown, nape, upperparts, and tail with a bluish gray gloss, and bright yellow underparts. Females have a rufous-chestnut forehead and mostly olive plumage with tawny-rufous underparts.
funFact: Builds a globe-shaped nest with a side entrance made from moss, roots, and plant fibers, typically placed in a clump of moss or epiphytes.

description: A very small wren with brownish plumage. The name troglodytes means hole dweller, referencing the bird's tendency to disappear into crevices when hunting insects or seeking shelter.
funFact: Formerly considered the same species as the Northern House Wren but was split based on genetic divergence, differences in vocalizations, and morphology.

description: Has chestnut brown upperparts with a darker crown, pale eyebrows, black-and-white streaked sides of the head and neck, and white underparts becoming buff on the lower belly. Wings and very short tail are barred with black.
funFact: Pairs often sing in duets, and the song has been compared to the opening bars of Beethoven's Fifth Symphony by ornithologist Luis Baptista.

description: A very large woodpecker, 31.5-36 cm long. Mainly black above with a red crest and whitish lines from the bill down the neck and shoulders. Underparts are whitish heavily barred with black. Adult males have a red line from the bill to the throat and a red forehead, while these features are black in females. The call is a loud, ringing wic-wic-wic.
funFact: Uses its strong bill to chip out large holes while searching for insects, especially ant and beetle larvae. Both males and females drum loudly on trees to communicate and establish territory.

description: A small songbird, 12.2 cm long. Males are brilliant violet-blue with black wings, tail and back, bright red legs, and a turquoise crown. Females and immatures are mainly green with paler, faintly streaked underparts. The call is a thin, high-pitched tsip. Often found in small groups feeding on insects, seeds, fruit, and nectar.
funFact: Males undergo a dramatic seasonal transformation, molting from their brilliant violet-blue breeding plumage into greenish eclipse plumage after the breeding season!

description: A medium-sized bird, 18.5-21 cm long. Adult males are mostly black with bright yellow patches on the shoulder, rump, belly, wing lining, and crissum. Females in the north have olive-yellow crown and upperparts with black face, throat, and wings; southern females resemble males. Both sexes have a slender, slightly decurved black bill with silvery base.
funFact: Builds a shallow pendant nest stitched to the underside of large leaves like heliconia or banana, making holes in the leaf and lacing fibers through them to hang the nest!

description: A colorful near-passerine bird with a black central crown surrounded by a blue band and a black eyemask. Features distinctive tail feathers with racket-shaped tips. The call is a low owl-like ooo-doot. Often sits motionless in dense forest habitat, making them difficult to spot despite their size. Eats insects, lizards, and fruit.
funFact: Nests in tunnels dug into banks, laying 3-4 white eggs. The unique racket-shaped tail feathers are created when the barbs naturally fall off the central portion of the tail!

description: A small tyrant flycatcher, 12.5-14 cm long. Has a dark olive crown, thin white eye-ring, and olive-green upperparts. Wings are dusky with yellowish-olive edges forming two wing bars. Throat is pale greenish gray, breast grayish greenish olive, and belly bright yellow. Features a wide flat bill with black upper mandible and pale gray lower mandible. Tends to be sluggish, peering slowly around from a perch.
funFact: Builds a distinctive pear-shaped bag nest with a tube entrance that slopes upward! Made from rootlets, fungal rhizomorphs, grass, and spider web, adults roost in it even outside breeding season.

description: A very large woodpecker, 35.5-38 cm long. Both sexes have black upperparts with vertical white or buffy white stripes on the sides of the neck forming a 'V' on the back. Throat and upper breast black, rest of underparts light yellowish buff with narrow black bars. Long ivory yellow chisel-like bill. Males have a red head with bushy crest; females have black forecrown and center of crown.
funFact: Requires large trees for foraging and disappears from heavily deforested areas. Its characteristic call sounds like a nasal squeeze toy: 'kip kip kip kip-hey'eh'eh'!

description: A tiny flycatcher, 9-10 cm long. Has a black crown and nape, white supercilium, yellow iris, gray face and upperparts, bright yellow underparts, and a short black tail. The wings have two yellowish wing bars. Features a distinctive long, flat, black bill that is pale at the base of the lower mandible. Often perches sideways on vertical stems.
funFact: Builds a hanging pouch nest with a side entrance, often decorated with bits of lichen and spider webs. Both parents incubate and feed the young!

description: A hummingbird, 13-16 cm long. Males weigh 5-7.5 g, females 4-6.5 g. Has greenish-brown upperparts and brownish to grayish underparts. Uppertail coverts have dark and light ochre bands, and central tail feathers are long with long white tips. Features a long decurved bill, with females having a shorter but more curved bill than males.
funFact: Males sing in communal leks of up to 25 birds and wiggle their long tails in display. Competitive lek singing can occupy half of the daylight hours to attract females!

description: A colorful trogon, 23-25 cm long, weighing 38-57 g. Males have a violet-blue head, neck, and upper breast with black face and throat. A pale yellow ring surrounds the eye, and a narrow white band separates the upper breast from the bright yellow lower breast and belly. Upperparts are metallic green with a violet-blue tail. Females are gray above with duller yellow underparts.
funFact: Nests in wasp, ant, or termite arboreal nests or holes in rotten trees. Its diet includes more fruit than most other trogons, supplemented with many types of insects!

description: A sparrow, 15-16 cm long, weighing 24-31 g. Has a mostly slate gray head with wide black stripes on each side of the crown, a black spot in front of the eye, and a long black stripe behind the eye. Upperparts, tail, and wings are bright olive green with canary yellow on the edge of the wings. Chin and throat are dull white, breast and sides grayish, belly white, and undertail coverts yellowish olive.
funFact: Builds a dome-shaped nest on the ground with a side entrance, made from plant fibers, leaves, and bark. Forages on the ground, usually singly or in pairs!

description: A small bird, 15.5-16.5 cm long, weighing 20-40 g. Has an indistinct pale ring around the eye on an otherwise dark brownish olive face. Upperparts and tail are dark brownish olive, wings are warmer brown. Throat and breast are brownish olive, belly and vent grayish olive. Both sexes have the same plumage.
funFact: Sings mostly in the early morning with a sequence of 2-4 clear, rich musical whistles! The female alone incubates eggs for 20-21 days and provisions nestlings by herself.

description: A bird, 15-18.5 cm long, weighing 24-32 g. Males are barred with alternating black and white throughout. Females have a cinnamon-rufous crest and upperparts with streaked black and white head and neck sides, buff chin, and ochraceous-buff underparts. Both sexes have a bushy crest and stout hooked bills.
funFact: Pairs vigorously defend year-round territories and sing duets while stretching their necks, bowing forward, and pumping their tails up and down!

description: A small flycatcher, 13-15 cm long, weighing 8.5-17 g. Has greenish upperparts and yellowish underparts with a dusky wash on the chest. Features a white or yellow eye ring, white or yellowish wing bars that contrast against black wings, and a broad flat bill. Upper mandible is dark while lower mandible is orange-pink.
funFact: Migrates from breeding grounds in Canada and northeastern US to winter in southern Mexico and Central America. Makes a cup nest in sphagnum moss on or near the ground!

description: A medium-sized hummingbird, 11-12 cm long. Males weigh 7.4-9 g, females 6-9.2 g. Males are unmistakable with dark blue head and chest, white belly and tail with black tips, and a white band on the nape separating the blue head from the bright green back. Females are highly variable with green upperparts, blue-green throat and breast with white scales, white belly, and mostly green tail with blue end.
funFact: Builds a shallow cup nest of plant down and cobweb on the upper surface of a leaf where another leaf provides a 'roof'! Several may feed in one tree and are aggressive to each other.

description: A medium-sized parrot, 24 cm long, weighing 225-250 g. Has a mostly green plumage with a distinctive white forecrown and throat with bare pink skin. The crown ranges from deep white to bluish white, with a magenta spot on the throat and bronze-green to bluish-green ear coverts. Chest and belly are yellowish-olive with bluish edges, while the undertail coverts are red. Features dark blue flight feathers and tail with red undertail coverts.
funFact: When disturbed, these parrots make a distinctive musky odor! They travel in flocks of 10-40 birds and can become very tame in captivity, though wild-caught birds are protected by CITES.

description: One of the smallest woodcreepers, 13-19 cm long, with a slim body and short straight bill. Has mostly olivaceous plumage with rufous tail and flight feathers. Unlike most other woodcreepers, has no streaks or spots. Males weigh 9-18 g, females 8.6-16 g. Often seen hitching up tree trunks and along branches from mid-level to subcanopy.
funFact: Often follows army ant swarms to catch insects fleeing from the ants, and frequently joins mixed-species feeding flocks where it may even form a core species!

description: A hummingbird, 11.5-13.5 cm long. Males have a bright violet crown, metallic green to bronze green upperparts, and bluish green uppertail coverts. Face is dull grayish white to gray with a white spot behind the eye. Underparts are brownish gray. Females similar but with duller blue crown and pale cinnamon wash on underparts. Bill is straight, longish, and stout.
funFact: Males sing the most complex song of any hummingbird, even comparable to songbirds! They display at leks and sing from bare twigs in the forest understory.

description: A small falcon, 23-30 cm long. Adults have blue-black head and upperparts with grayish edges. Throat, upper breast, and neck sides are white to buff; rest of breast is black with fine white bars. Belly, thighs, and undertail coverts are chestnut-rufous. Long wings and longish tail with square tip. Bright yellow cere and bare skin around eye, orange-yellow legs and feet.
funFact: Named for their habit of hunting bats! They are most active around dawn and dusk, often hunting well past sunset from high, open perches.

description: A small-medium woodpecker, 17-19 cm long, weighing 42-68 g. Males have golden yellow forehead and red crown/nape. Females have white forehead, black central crown, and red hindcrown/nape. Both have black around eye extending down neck with white line behind eye. Barred upper back, white lower back. Whitish throat and breast with olive-buff tinge, buffish white underparts with wavy bars and red belly spot.
funFact: Very active and agile forager that sometimes clings to the underside of branches! Feeds on everything from insects to fruits to flower nectar.

description: A sexually dimorphic songbird. Males are dark blue with lighter blue eyebrows and shoulder patches on wings, and lighter blue forehead above beak. Females have dark brown plumage with slight reddish hue. Prefer to stay hidden in dense vegetation. Song consists of about six whistles with decreasing pitch.
funFact: Often not seen because they prefer to stay hidden in vegetation - their presence is usually known only by their distinctive song of about six whistles with decreasing pitch!

description: A large tyrant flycatcher, 18 cm long, weighing 40 g, with big head, hooked and slightly upturned bill, and upright stance. Head olive-green streaked with black, back chestnut or olive, rump bright yellow, tail brown. Wings dark brown with two pale wing bars. Whitish or yellow throat and yellow breast variably streaked darker. Belly white becoming yellow near tail. Red iris. Very variable plumage.
funFact: Active, aggressive, and noisy! Will pursue prey on foot and follow army ant columns. Known for its loud 'beat-it, beat-it' call and plaintive whistled song.

description: A hummingbird 11.5-13 cm long, weighing 8-10 g. Mostly bronzy green with a grayish buff belly and distinctive scaly appearance created by buffy edges on underpart feathers. The tail is mostly bronzy with white tips on outer feathers. Has a mostly black bill with pink base.
funFact: Males display for females at leks during breeding season. This hummingbird aggressively defends patches of flowers and dominates smaller hummingbirds at feeding sites!

description: A large pigeon, 26.5 cm long, weighing 150 g. Mainly wine-purple in color with a black bill and purple-red eyes and legs. The belly is browner and the back is more olive-brown, with blackish tail and flight feathers. Forages in tree tops for mistletoe, fruits and berries.
funFact: Named for its proportionally short bill compared to other pigeons in its genus. Has a complex, loud and high-pitched call that is slower and more complex than its close relative, the ruddy pigeon.

description: A distinctive blackbird about 21.5 cm long. Both males and females are entirely black with a characteristic pale yellow bill that is chisel-shaped at the tip. Has a pale yellow iris and gray legs. Often feeds like a woodpecker, hammering and probing bamboo stems and rotten wood to extract insects.
funFact: Pairs duet together with complex vocalizations, and this bird feeds like a woodpecker by hammering into bamboo and inserting its closed bill into substrates then opening it to expose hidden prey!

description: A small bird, 10-10.7 cm long. Adult males have an olive-green back with black face and breast marked by a distinctive bright yellow throat, supercilia, and lower eyelid spot. The rest of the underparts are greyish olive. Females are dull olive-green above and paler grey below with a weaker yellow face pattern.
funFact: Builds a roughly globular nest with a side entrance placed usually less than 30 cm above the ground among grass or weeds. During courtship, males vibrate their wings while singing just inches away from the female!

description: A thick-billed songbird found from Mexico to Panama. Members of the saltator genus have robust bills, relatively long tails, and strong legs. This species has grey upperparts with cinnamon-colored underparts. Like other saltators, it forages for seeds, fruits, and insects in the canopy and mid-levels of trees.
funFact: In El Salvador, this bird is affectionately known as 'dichosofui' after its elaborate call, which sounds like the Spanish phrase '¡dichoso fui!' meaning 'I was happy!'

description: A medium-sized woodpecker, 18-23 cm long. Both sexes have mostly green upperparts with a bronze tinge and pale buffy yellow underparts barred with blackish olive. Males have a slate gray crown with a red border and nape and a wide red malar stripe. Females have red only on the nape. Forages from the mid-level to the canopy for ants, termites, and wood-boring beetles.
funFact: This woodpecker has been observed eating Müllerian bodies - specialized food structures that Cecropia plants produce to feed their symbiotic ants!

description: The mangrove swallow is a passerine bird found from Mexico through Central America to Panama. It has iridescent blue-green upperparts, white underparts, a white rump, and a black tail. It can be identified by the distinctive supraloral white streak near its eye, a feature shared only with two other Tachycineta species. Highly territorial when breeding, it nests in cavities near water and feeds on unusually large aerial prey for its size, including dragonflies and bees.
funFact: The mangrove swallow feeds on unusually large prey for its size, including dragonflies and bees, and typically forages within 100 meters of its nest when feeding its young.

description: A compact, short-tailed bird with a heavy hooked bill and orange legs. Males have a black crown, wings, and tail, a white head, neck, and breast contrasting with a bright yellow belly. Females are olive-green with a yellow belly. Males perform elaborate lekking displays on the forest floor, using their modified wings to produce loud snapping sounds.
funFact: Males clear a patch of forest floor up to 120 cm across to bare earth for their display courts, leaping to and fro between thin upright sticks while giving loud wing snaps.

description: A medium-sized bird of prey with creamy white underparts, a broad black face mask that wraps around the neck, and a small bushy crest. The wings and back are dusky black, and the tail has bold white bands. It has short, rounded wings and a long tail. Known for its loud, far-carrying laughing call, it is often seen perched conspicuously for hours watching for prey.
funFact: The laughing falcon specializes in hunting snakes, including venomous coral snakes, and its scientific name Herpetotheres cachinnans means 'snake mower' and 'laughing aloud'.

description: A medium-sized flycatcher with a small head and bushy crest. It has brownish olive upperparts, whitish wing bars, a pale gray throat, olive gray breast, and yellow belly. Common in open and semi-open habitats from Mexico through Central and South America.
funFact: Its dawn song has been described as "spud-deeer, spud-deer-dzz, spud-deer", repeated over and over.

description: A very small hummingbird with dull iridescent green upperparts, pale rufous rump, buff belly, and a blackish "bandit-mask" face with whitish stripes. It has a long, decurved bill and is found in wooded habitats from Mexico through Central America to northwestern South America.
funFact: It is a "trap-line" feeder, visiting a regular circuit of flowers, and has been observed piercing flower bases to access nectar that would otherwise be out of reach.

description: A large, predominantly white vulture with gray to black ruff, flight, and tail feathers. The head and neck are bald with colorful skin in yellow, orange, blue, purple, and red, and it has a distinctive orange fleshy caruncle on its beak.
funFact: The king vulture was revered in Maya mythology, often portrayed as a god carrying messages between humans and the other gods, and appears frequently in Maya codices.

description: A very small finch with a stubby bill, about 10 cm long. Males have a yellow forecrown, glossy blue-black head and upperparts, and bright yellow underparts. Females are olive-yellow with grayish crowns. It is found in open and semi-open habitats from Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: The scrub euphonia feeds primarily on mistletoe berries, making it an important seed disperser for this parasitic plant.

description: A large chestnut bird with a blackish head and rump, bright yellow tail, bare blue cheek patch, pink wattle, and a long black-and-red bill. Males are nearly twice the size of females. It is found in the Caribbean lowlands from Mexico to central Panama.
funFact: The Montezuma oropendola builds hanging woven nests up to 180 cm long, and a single colony can contain as many as 172 nests in one tree.

description: A very small flycatcher, 9.5-10.5 cm long, with a distinctive downcurved bill. It has a pale gray head, olive upperparts, pale gray throat and breast with olive streaks, and a pale lemon-yellow belly. It inhabits dense undergrowth from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: The northern bentbill's call is described as a rapid toad-like trill, and it gets its name from its distinctive downcurved bill.

description: A medium-sized gray songbird, 23 to 25.5 cm long, with a whitish eyebrow, dark stripe through the eye, off-white underparts, and blackish wings with two white wing bars. It has a long dark tail with white feather tips and a slim slightly curved black bill. Common in open habitats and around human habitation throughout Belize and Central America.
funFact: Despite being a mockingbird, the tropical mockingbird rarely mimics other species, unlike its well-known northern relative.

description: A small antbird, 13 to 15 cm long. Males are mostly slate gray with a white patch between the scapulars and white tips on the wing coverts and outer tail feathers. Females have tawny-tinged olive-gray upperparts and tawny-buff underparts. Both sexes have distinctive rich chocolate-brown irises. Inhabits dense understorey of forest edges, clearings, and secondary growth from Mexico through Central America.
funFact: This antbird regularly attends army ant swarms to catch insects fleeing from the ants, and both parents share incubation and feeding duties.

description: A thrush-like forest bird, 15 to 19 cm long, with a dusky brown crown, bare bluish skin around the eye, black face and throat, rufous sides of the neck, and uniform gray underparts. Walks slowly on the forest floor with its tail cocked, found in humid forests from sea level to 1,800 m from Mexico to Honduras.
funFact: The Mayan antthrush walks deliberately on the forest floor with its tail cocked upright like a little rail, and pasture gaps wider than 50 meters are significant barriers to its movement.

description: A very small bird, 12 to 13 cm long, with a notably long thin bill and a short frequently cocked tail. Upperparts are grey-brown with rufous on the sides of the head, white throat shading to buff underparts, and a black tail with white tips. Forages actively in vegetation for insects and spiders.
funFact: This bird's long, thin bill is perfect for picking insects and spiders from foliage, and it frequently wags and cocks its short black tail with white-tipped feathers.

description: A large trogon, 30 to 35.5 cm long. Males have an orange-red bill, metallic green upperparts and breast, deep red belly, and slate gray undertail. Females are gray where males are green. Often seen sitting motionless in the forest midstory, from Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Ecuador.
funFact: This trogon often follows white-faced capuchin monkeys to snatch insects they flush, and nests in cavities excavated in active termite nests high in trees.

description: A small flycatcher, 12.5 to 14 cm long, with greenish olive upperparts, buffy ochraceous edges on the wings, and rich cinnamon buff underparts. Forages at middle levels of the forest, hover-gleaning fruit and insects from a perch. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Males court at leks, singing and flashing their wings, and the female builds a pear-shaped moss-covered nest with a side entrance suspended from a vine.

description: A tiny bird, just 10 cm long. Males are velvety black with a brilliant red cap and bright yellow thighs. Females are olive green above with paler greenish-yellow underparts. Found in the understory of humid forests from Mexico to Ecuador, often near fruiting trees.
funFact: The male's courtship display includes shuffling rapidly backwards along a branch like a moonwalk, earning it the nickname 'the Michael Jackson bird.' Its digestive system can process fruit in less than 18 minutes.

description: A medium-sized warbler, about 13 cm long, with olive upperparts and bright yellow underparts. The male has a gray crown and nape with black restricted to the lores and behind the eye, unlike the full face mask of other yellowthroats. The female is similar but lacks the black face markings. Found in open grassy and shrubby habitats from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: Unlike most yellowthroats, the gray-crowned yellowthroat has scarcely any black on its face, with the black usually restricted to just the lores and behind the eye.

description: A small flycatcher, 13-14 cm long, with greenish olive upperparts, a concealed yellow crown stripe, and pale yellow underparts. It has a whitish gray supercilium and partial white eyering. Found in a wide variety of wooded habitats from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: The greenish elaenia is the only species in the genus Myiopagis found from Mexico through Central America and across much of South America, with 10 recognized subspecies.

description: A large pigeon, 30-32 cm long, with a dull purple head and breast, copper gloss on the nape, and pale grey underparts. The bill is black and the legs, iris, and eyering are red. Found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina in forest edges and partially open areas.
funFact: Its call is a distinctive row of soft notes — "kuk kuk croo-ooo" — where the initial short "kuk" is characteristic of the cayennensis group of Patagioenas pigeons.

description: A large kingfisher, about 30 cm long, with dark bronzy green upperparts, a white collar, and a long heavy black bill. Males have a rich rufous breast while females are green across the breast. Found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina along rivers and lakes.
funFact: The Amazon kingfisher hunts by plunging into water from a perch, but also hovers like a helicopter before diving for fish.

description: A striking raptor, 46-56 cm long, with entirely white head, body, and underwings contrasting with black upper wings and a short black tail with a broad white band. The bill is black and legs are yellow. Females are larger than males. Found from southern Mexico through Central America to the Amazon basin.
funFact: The white hawk often follows foraging groups of tufted capuchin monkeys and coatis to snatch prey startled by these animals.

description: A large rail, 33-40 cm long, with slate gray crown and hindneck, a chestnut patch on the nape, grayish olive back, white chin, and pale tawny cinnamon underparts fading to black on the lower belly. It has a thick dark yellow bill, deep red eye, and red legs. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica in dense wetlands and forests.
funFact: Its song is a rapid, crazed-sounding series of popping and clucking notes, most often heard at twilight and at night.

description: A tiny owl, only about 15 cm long, stocky with disproportionately large feet and talons. Its crown has elongated white streaks and its underparts are heavily streaked white. Two dark spots on the nape resemble false eyes. Color varies from grey-brown to rich rufous. Found in semi-open wooded habitats from the southern US to Argentina.
funFact: The two dark spots on the back of its head look like eyes, a clever defense that helps fool both predators and small birds trying to mob it.

description: A large parrot, 38-41 cm long, mainly green with a distinctive whitish powdery tinge on the back and nape, a short square tail, and a red speculum on the wing. It has a broad white eye-ring and a bi-colored tail that is paler yellow at the tip. Found in humid forests from southern Mexico to the Amazon basin.
funFact: The name "mealy" comes from the whitish, flour-like powder on its back and nape — "farinosa" in Latin means "sprinkled with flour."

description: A striking wetland bird, 21-24 cm long, with a chestnut-maroon body, black head and neck, bright yellow bill, and a yellow fleshy forehead shield. Its most remarkable feature is its extremely long toes that allow it to walk on floating lily pads. Females are significantly larger than males. Found from Mexico to Panama in freshwater wetlands.
funFact: Its enormous feet let it walk on floating lily pads, earning it the nickname "Jesus bird" for appearing to walk on water.

description: The white-whiskered puffbird is about 18 cm (7.1 in) long. Both sexes have bristles around the base of the large bill and distinctive white 'whiskers' made of feather tufts. Males have brown to chestnut brown upperparts while females have grayer crowns and olive to brown upperparts.
funFact: This puffbird hunts using a watch-and-wait technique, sitting motionless before darting to catch large invertebrates and small vertebrates like frogs and lizards.

description: The crimson-collared tanager averages 19-20 cm (7.5-8 in) long. Adult plumage is black with a vivid red collar covering the nape, neck, and breast, and red tail coverts. Its bill is striking pale blue and legs are blue-gray. Females average slightly duller than males but are sometimes indistinguishable.
funFact: Its specific epithet sanguinolentus means 'bloodied' in Latin, referring to its striking red plumage.

description: The bare-throated tiger heron is 80 cm (31 in) long, a large wading bird with a bare greenish-yellow to orange throat. Adults have a black crown, light grey sides of the head, and blackish upperparts narrowly barred with buff. Juveniles are buff coarsely barred with black. Its flight is heavy and it is often found motionless along river and lake banks.
funFact: Males give a booming hrrrowwr! call at sunset, during which the beak opens wide and undulations can be seen along the throat.

description: The rufous-tailed jacamar is 25 cm (10 in) long with a 5 cm (2 in) black bill. It is metallic green above with mainly orange underparts and a green breast band. Males have a white throat while females have a buff throat. It sits with its bill tilted up, flying out to catch insects in mid-air.
funFact: This bird can distinguish between edible and unpalatable butterflies mainly by their body shape.

description: A large seed-eating bird with a black face, yellow head, neck and breast, and olive back, wings and tail. It inhabits the canopy and middle levels of dense wet forests and tall second growth in Central American lowlands.
funFact: Forms noisy flocks of up to 20 birds, often mixing with honeycreepers and tanagers in mixed-species feeding flocks.

description: A powerful eagle with black plumage, barred wings, and a long narrow tail with distinctive grey bars. It hunts relatively large prey including monkeys, rodents, opossums, and reptiles from central Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: Its popular name in Brazil is Gavião-pega-macaco, meaning hawk catching-monkey, as it frequently preys on monkeys.

description: A striking tyrant flycatcher with white underparts, gray upperparts, a black cap, and an extremely long forked tail. Found from southern Mexico to Argentina in open habitats including pastures, savannas, and forest edges.
funFact: Can reach speeds up to 105 km/h (65 mph), using its long forked tail for quick turns and stops while pursuing flying insects.

description: A small, robust bird with a black conical bill. Males vary in plumage by subspecies from entirely black to black with white collars and bellies, while females are olive-brown above and paler below. Commonly found in semi-open areas from southern Mexico through Central America.
funFact: The subspecies found in Belize (Sporophila c. corvina) has entirely black males apart from a small white wing patch.

description: A large, secretive forest falcon with a dark back, white neck and breast, and long tail. It has three color morphs (pale, tawny, and dark) and is a powerful predator that hunts mammals, birds, and reptiles in dense tropical forests from Mexico to South America.
funFact: Its distinctive low, almost human-like ow or ahr call can be heard at great distances through the dense forest canopy.

description: A small tanager with a long decurved bill. Males are mainly blue-tinged green with a black head and bright yellow bill, while females are grass-green without the black head. Found in the forest canopy from southern Mexico to Brazil.
funFact: Unlike other honeycreepers, it depends less on nectar — fruit and seeds make up about 60% of its diet.

description: A large wren with a brown-grey crown, black nape, and heavily barred upperparts and tail with black, tawny, and white. The breast is white with heavy black spots and the belly is cinnamon. Found from Mexico to Ecuador.
funFact: After breeding, family groups of 4-12 birds sleep together in dormitory nests used throughout the year.

description: A slender swift with long narrow wings and a long forked tail, mainly black with a white throat and upper breast. Found from southern Mexico through Central America and South America, often flying very fast and high above the forest canopy.
funFact: Its nest is a long tube made of plant material felted with saliva, wider at the top with the entrance at the base, attached to a vertical surface or tree branch.

description: A medium-sized hummingbird with a slightly decurved black bill. Males have metallic bronze-green upperparts and a velvety black stripe down the throat and chest, while females have white-tipped outer tail feathers. Found from Mexico through Central America in semi-open habitats.
funFact: Despite being a tropical species, it has wandered as far north as Wisconsin and North Carolina as a vagrant to the United States.

description: A seed-eating tanager with a slate-grey head, white supercilium, olive-green upperparts, and a buff throat edged with black. Its thick convex black bill is distinctive. Found from southeastern Mexico through Central America to western Ecuador and northeastern Brazil.
funFact: Males duet melodiously with a warbled "cheery cheery" answered by "cheery to you."

description: A tiny flycatcher with a slate-grey crown, white forehead, bright olive upperparts, and bright yellow underparts with faint olive streaks on the breast. Found in the canopy and edges of humid forests from southern Mexico to western Panama.
funFact: Despite being a flycatcher, its active foraging behavior resembles that of a warbler or vireo as it gleans arthropods from leaves and twigs.

description: A small flycatcher with brown upperparts, white throat, yellow breast, a short stubby tail, and a broad flat bill. It lacks a crown, which distinguishes it from other Platyrinchus species. Found in the undergrowth of tropical forests from southern Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: Its greatly reduced or completely absent crown distinguishes it from all other members of the genus Platyrinchus.

description: A striking oriole that is mostly yellow with a black back, lower face, and upper breast, and black wings with yellow epaulets. The tail is black with yellow margins above and appears almost entirely yellow from below. It is the only oriole with prominent yellow in the tail.
funFact: This is the only oriole with prominent yellow in the tail, which gives the species its name.

description: A small dove with striking sexual dimorphism. Males are blue-grey with black-spotted wings and paler underparts. Females are grey-brown with a ruddy brown back, chestnut rump and tail, and chestnut-brown wing spots. Both have red or yellow irises and a green bare eyering.
funFact: In most regions where it occurs, a blue-grey and a brown bird flying through the trees together is almost certainly this species.

description: A medium-sized woodcreeper with a brown head, buffy supercilium, bare gray skin around the eye, dark olive-brown upperparts, cinnamon-rufous tail, and tawny flight feathers. Its underparts are pale buff on the throat becoming cinnamon on the belly. It has a straight bill and a ruffled nape.
funFact: This species is a near-obligate follower of army ant swarms, capturing arthropods that flee from the advancing ants.

description: A large sparrow with a chestnut crown streaked with black, brown upperparts with bold black streaks, chestnut wings and tail, and grayish buff underparts with a dark stripe on the side of the throat. Both sexes share the same plumage. Found from Mexico to Costa Rica.
funFact: Its song has been described as a rich, liquid "cheet-cheet-swing-you!" or "sweet-sweet-to'you!"

description: A medium-to-large raptor with a striking rufous cowl, black crown and long erectile crest, bold black barring on white underparts, and blackish upperparts with white-tipped shoulders. Juveniles are mostly white-headed with dark brown upperparts. Found in tall primary forests from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: The juvenile gray-bellied hawk closely resembles the adult ornate hawk-eagle, likely a case of mimicry where a less powerful species mimics a more powerful raptor to avoid attack.

description: A small cardinalid with striking sexual dimorphism. The male is brilliant blue with a sky blue forehead and cheek, blackish blue upperparts, and deep blue underparts. The female is dull brownish overall with a reddish tinge to the belly. Found in dense vegetation from Mexico to Nicaragua.
funFact: The male's song has been described as "a sweet, rather sad warble."

description: A medium-sized owl with a round head and no ear tufts. The crown, nape, and back are mottled brown, the facial disc is pale brown, and the underparts are off-white with bold vertical brown streaks. The eyes are dark brown. Found in a variety of wooded habitats from Mexico to Argentina.
funFact: The mottled owl shows the greatest degree of sexual dimorphism of any owl species, with females considerably larger than males.

description: A small sparrow-sized crake with bright chestnut plumage, a gray head with blackish crown, dark grey ear-coverts, and paler chin and belly. It has a short black conical bill, red iris, and distinctive olive-green legs and feet. Found in wet lowlands from Mexico to northwestern Costa Rica.
funFact: After no sightings for two decades in Costa Rica, it was listed as possibly extinct there until rediscovered in 2007 at the La Selva Biological Research Station.

description: A small greenlet with a yellowish forehead, yellow-brown crown, olive-brown upperparts, dull gray face, and ochraceous breast becoming bright yellow on the belly. It has a pale gray iris and moves actively through the forest understory, often hanging upside-down while foraging. Found from Mexico to Ecuador.
funFact: Its genus name Tunchiornis derives from 'El Tunche,' the whistling jungle spirit of Amazonian mythology, referring to its simple whistled song.

description: A striking tanager with strong sexual dimorphism. The male is mostly yellow to orange-yellow with a black hood, wings, and tail. The female is olive-yellow below and olive-green to brownish above with a grayish head. Both have a relatively large, hooked bill. Found from southeastern Mexico to central Honduras.
funFact: It is replaced geographically by its close relative the White-throated Shrike-Tanager further south in Central America.

description: A very small swift with a cigar-shaped body, long crescentic wings, and a short bluntly squared-off tail. The head, upperparts, and wings are dusky black, while the underparts, rump, and tail coverts are greyish brown with a paler grey throat. Found from Alaska through Central America to Venezuela, often flying in flocks over forests and open areas.
funFact: Named for William Sansom Vaux (pronounced 'vox'), this swift has a more varied call repertoire than others in its genus, mixing chattering, buzzes, squeaks, and chips.

description: The largest hummingbird in Mexico and Central America at 13-15 cm. Males have a dusky crown with bluish green gloss, metallic violet blue underparts, and metallic green lower back. Females have metallic green upperparts, a violet blue throat, and gray underparts with a whiter belly. Found in humid evergreen forests and gardens from Mexico to Panama.
funFact: The violet sabrewing is the largest hummingbird in Mexico and Central America, measuring 13 to 15 cm long, and males are known to aggressively defend flower patches from other hummingbirds.

description: A small warbler about 12.7 cm long with plain olive-gray upperparts, a bright yellow chest and throat, and a white belly. Its distinctive facial pattern includes a rufous cap, white eyebrow-line, dark eye-line, and white malar marking. It has a stout bill, round stubby wings, and a long tail often held at a high angle.
funFact: Despite its name, the rufous-capped warbler does not actually warble — its courtship song is a rapid, accelerating series of chipping notes.

description: The smallest woodcreeper at 13-16 cm, with a distinctive short upturned wedge-shaped bill. It has a russet-brown crown, rich brown upperparts, cinnamon-rufous rump and tail, and olive-brown underparts with fulvous spots and streaks on the breast. It hitches up tree trunks in the lower to middle levels of the forest, often in mixed-species flocks.
funFact: The wedge-billed woodcreeper is the only member of its genus and is the smallest of all woodcreepers, easily recognized by its short wedge-shaped bill.

description: A small, secretive ground bird about 21-24 cm long with unbarred sooty-brown plumage, a greyish head, whitish foreneck, and cinnamon-buff underparts. It has a very short tail and can be grey, olive, or yellow legs. More often heard than seen, it inhabits tropical lowland forests from southern Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: The little tinamou is more closely related to ostriches and emus (both ratites) than to quail or grouse, despite looking similar — it belongs to the ancient group of palaeognath birds.

description: A very small dove about 14.5-16 cm long, possibly the smallest columbid by mass. It has dark grey to brown plumage, paler wings with dark violet spots, rufous underwings, a grey bill, pink legs, and reddish eyes. Unlike the similar common ground dove, it lacks scaling on the head and neck. Found in open dry areas from southern Mexico through Central and South America.
funFact: The plain-breasted ground dove may be the smallest of all pigeons and doves by mass, yet it can be found across an enormous range spanning 18.8 million square kilometers from Mexico to Argentina.

description: A large raptor 56-64 cm long with very broad wings, mainly black plumage, a short white tail with a broad black tip, a black bill, and yellow legs and cere. Immature birds are dark brown above with buff underparts and dark spots. Its call is a distinctive piping ooo-wheeeeee. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern Argentina.
funFact: One Great Black Hawk became a famous vagrant in 2018, traveling from Texas to Maine — the first record in the ABA area — where it survived a winter before being rescued during a snowstorm.

description: A small antbird 10-12.5 cm long with a long graduated tail. Males are mostly black with a concealed white patch between the shoulders and white spots on the wing coverts. Females have a blackish gray head and upperparts, black throat, and rufous-chestnut underparts. Found actively foraging in vine tangles and forest edges from Mexico through Central America to South America.
funFact: The dot-winged antwren is the only member of its genus Microrhopias, and the male's striking black plumage with white wing dots makes it one of the most distinctive antwrens in its range.

description: A very small tyrant flycatcher 9-10 cm long with a gray crown and nape, olive back and tail, yellowish wing bars, and grayish white underparts with streaks on the throat and breast. Found in dense scrubby vegetation at forest edges and in secondary growth from Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Its nest is pear-shaped with a side entrance, woven from moss and plant fibers, and suspended from the tip of a branch about 3 meters above the ground.

description: A small furnariid 11-13 cm long with dull brown upperparts, a conspicuous buff supercilium, wide white malar stripe, and extensive rufous in the wings and tail. Its underparts are plain dull grayish brown with light spotting on the foreneck and breast. It has a distinctive wedge-shaped, slightly upturned bill. Found from southern Mexico through Central America to northern South America.
funFact: Uses its wedge-shaped, upturned bill to chisel and pry arthropod prey from dead branches, often foraging while hanging upside down like a nuthatch.

description: A small hummingbird 7.5-9.3 cm long. Males have brilliant golden green upperparts and underparts, a red bill with black tip, and a long forked blue-black tail. Females have metallic green upperparts, light gray underparts, and a grayish white stripe behind the eye. Found in semi-open landscapes from Mexico through Central America.
funFact: Holds its tail partly open and wags it while feeding on nectar, often visiting small flowers that are ignored by other hummingbirds.

description: The smallest motmot at 16.5-18 cm long. It has a green crown, rufous neck, green back and rump, a blue supercilium, and a black mask with a white stripe below. Its throat is white, breast greenish with light streaks, and belly white. Found in humid evergreen forest from southern Mexico through Central America into Colombia.
funFact: The tody motmot is the only species in its genus Hylomanes and is the smallest of all motmots, named for its resemblance to the todies of the Caribbean.

description: A medium-sized passerine 17-19 cm long. The male is dull reddish brown with a brighter red throat and breast, featuring a black-bordered scarlet crown stripe that is raised when excited. The female is yellowish brown with a yellow throat and yellow-buff crown stripe. Found from Mexico through Central America to Paraguay and northern Argentina.
funFact: Frequently follows army ant columns to snatch invertebrate prey startled by the ants, and also trails South American coatis on their feeding excursions.

description: The largest swift in its range at 20-21.5 cm long with a 45-55 cm wingspan. Mostly black plumage with a distinctive white collar that circles the neck, broadest on the breast. The tail is slightly forked. Found from Mexico through Central America and the Caribbean to South America.
funFact: White-collared swifts attain flying speeds of 70-100 km/h (43-62 mph) and often feed in flocks of 200 or more, sometimes alongside other swift species.

description: A tyrant flycatcher 15-17 cm long with olive-green upperparts, a bold white eye-ring, and a large wide flat bill. The throat and upper breast are dusky olive-green becoming paler with yellowish streaks on the lower breast, with a pale yellow belly. Found from Mexico through Central America into Colombia.
funFact: Builds a large pear-shaped nest with a downward-sloping tunnel entrance, suspended from a twig or vine over open space or water, and adults roost in the nest year-round.

description: A hummingbird 10-11.5 cm long with a bright metallic blue crown, metallic bronze nape and back, and white underparts with metallic bronze green sides to the breast. Found from Mexico through Belize, Guatemala, El Salvador into Nicaragua.
funFact: In urban areas, azure-crowned hummingbirds have been known to build their nests on utility wires, a remarkable adaptation to human-altered environments.

description: A tiny songbird with blue-gray upperparts, whitish underparts, and a long black tail with white outer feathers that is often held cocked. Males have a black cap with a striking white eyebrow; females have a blue-gray cap with a white eyebrow. Constantly in motion while foraging. Found from Mexico to northwestern Peru.
funFact: Formerly considered a subspecies of the Tropical Gnatcatcher, the White-browed Gnatcatcher was elevated to full species status based on genetic studies showing it is distinct.

description: A striking raptor 51-61 cm long with a white head, neck and body, black wings with a white leading edge, and a brownish tail barred black-grey with a white tip. It has a small black crest on top of the head and black around the eyes. Found from southern Mexico to northern Argentina.
funFact: Each black-and-white hawk-eagle requires a hunting territory of about 3,500 acres (1,400 hectares) and has been known to snatch prey in mid-air that was mobbing it.

description: A spinetail 13-16 cm long with sepia brown upperparts, chestnut wings and tail, a slate black throat with white streaks, and a deep cinnamon rufous breast and sides. Found in dense vegetation from Mexico through Belize, Guatemala, El Salvador, and Honduras.
funFact: Builds a dome-shaped nest of thorny sticks with a tunnel entrance, sometimes half again as long as it is high, thatched with bark and leaf stems.

description: A slender hummingbird 9-13 cm long with bright emerald green upperparts, pure white underparts, and a long pointed tail with blue-black central and white outer feathers. The male has a metallic violet forecrown. Found from Mexico through Central America to northern Peru.
funFact: Unlike most hummingbirds, the purple-crowned fairy does not catch insects in flight — it hovers and gleans them from foliage, and will pierce the base of flowers to 'rob' nectar.

description: A large woodcreeper 25-29 cm long with a cinnamon-brown face, rufescent crown, olive-brown back with black barring, cinnamon-rufous tail, and golden to cinnamon-buff underparts with narrow black barring. Has a long tail and medium-length straight bill. Found from Mexico through Central America to Colombia and Ecuador.
funFact: Often follows army ant swarms to feed on prey disturbed by the ants, perching on near-vertical trunks and making short flights to pick prey from the ground, trunks, and foliage.

description: A medium-sized woodcreeper 17.5-20.5 cm long with dark rufous to chestnut-brown upperparts, a brighter reddish crown, rufous chestnut wings and tail, cinnamon-buff throat, and rufous brown underparts with a paler belly. Has a straight bill and short tail. Found from Mexico to northern Colombia and Venezuela.
funFact: A near-obligate follower of army ant swarms, it clings to trunks up to 10 meters high and captures arthropod prey disturbed by the ants, but never feeds on the ants themselves.

description: A large dove 23.5-26 cm long with a pale bluish gray forehead and crown, pinkish buff face, white throat and belly, and pale pink breast. Upperparts are olive brown with a rufous wash. Outer tail feathers are black with white tips. Found from Mexico to Colombia.
funFact: The gray-headed dove, gray-fronted dove, pallid dove, Azuero dove, and Grenada dove were once all thought to be a single species.
